comment: I came to Malaysia 40 years ago to marry a Malay woman I met while studying in the US. I worked for 35 years here, and am now helping to raise our Malaysian grandchildren. Looking back, I have to say I won the lottery. I would never live anywhere else but Malaysia. The only thing I miss is the crystal blue skies of spring and autumn.

Going to KL really changed my perspective about Western cities being the best ones. 
What I loved about KL was people. Everyone's very nice.
I am a single western woman.  I travelled to KL and Malaysia/ Indonesia independently. No issues at all.

As a Malaysian living overseas .. it amuses me to see videos like yours . I think one thing you have to highlight Malaysia is not Kuala Lumpur or Penang ?? There are parts of Malaysia which is not as urbanised as KL, Penang or JB.. yes.  It takes awhile to “crack” into an inner circle  but if you stay authentic and get to know the locals’ way of thinking ..  before you know it, you got yourself a tribe 😊
I am a Malaysian, used to nomad throughout the Americas and Europe. Yeah KL is pretty fucking awesome.

I grew up in Europe but never liked the harsh winters we used to have those days. In 1990 I sold my 50% of our business to my partner, sold everything else I had, and hopped onto my trusted (motor)bike with 10kg of stuff in a bag. 15 months later I crossed from Thailand into Malaysia and since that time I lived here. I married here, brought up 2 children and still love it here! Best.Decision.Ever.Period

Also there´s essentially 3 cultures in one city; Malay, Chinese and Indian, I have never seen diversity like this in a city before.

Malaysian here, speaking for the Malays we have a tradition of “adab” (loosely translated as etiquette or manners) which we hope newcomers take the time to learn. Despite the cosmopolitan nature of our cities, like any other people we expect a bit of respect and integration from newcomers. Even at the multicultural national level there are some topics which are avoided in discourse between communities. As long as newcomers observe these sensitivities, Malaysians are very welcoming.

I see my millionaire relatives in Singapore still working hard every day even after 60, running a hawker stall, selling their car and commuting by MRT and LRT to work. Meanwhile, my parents retired about 15 years ago at around 50 with less than RM50k in savings. My sibling and I only give them RM2000 per month, yet they live quite happily in JB, spending time with friends every day and traveling once a year. Life is short, even billionaires continue working hard every day. We cannot surpass everyone, but we can choose to live happily as long as our basic needs are met.

People tend to assume salaries are about comparable dollar for dollar.  This is understandable at the lower-end where differences are not significant enough to be noticeable.  As HR responsible for SG/MY, I have analysed the confidential pay scales of both countries across a variety of industries and the general difference in salaries for jobs of similar size is 1.6 : 1 (MY:SG), on average.  Meaning, if you have a job that pays SGD1k p.m. in Singapore, the same "sized" job will be pay RM1.6k in Malaysia.  So, if consumer goods cost 2-3x more in MY but you are paid only 1.6x more, that is why people feel money tends to go further in SG.  The bigger numbers in MY also tend to push you to the higher tier of taxes, thus further reducing your nett income.

The system isn’t about privilege for the native, that’s what the uneducated thinks because it’s simpler to understand. It isn’t about who invited whom. We are all equal, we are all Malaysians. The incentives are about economics. As part of the British divide and conquer tactics, the races are segregated to prevent unity against the ruler minority. Natives were kept in the village, the Chinese in the mining/trade, the educated Indians in bureaucracy and the uneducated Indians in the plantations. Since different sector benefited by different amount economically, there was economic inequality between the races and the animosity in the country lead to racial riot in 1969. Economic policies were introduced by the government voted by the majority race who created incentives to try level the economics disparity. It’s a lot closer now although it’s quite obvious if you look around you in Malaysia which ethnicity is still head and shoulders above everyone, despite the economic preference for another ethnicity. People like to believe that this is due to their inherent characteristic to overcome challenges but the truth is, they benefited from generational and systematic wealth advantage for the original British policy. When your family can afford to send you to the best schools, you get given great jobs by your family/your racially identical neighbours or the systemic racism of “mandarin speakers only” recruitment policy, it’s not hard to continue the economic success of your ethnic group vs the other group who had no opportunity for education, no networking and no money. The government offered incentives that promoted inclusivity such as minimum quota into education to make sure the majority race isn’t left behind in education. Same with businesses. The government created networking opportunity by sheltering a tiny portion of the economy so that new start ups can eventually participate in the broader economy. Evidently it’s successful. Beside the grumblings of the uneducated and the selfish, the races live in harmony and the country thrives economically compared to its neighbours bar SG. Countries that did not practice affirmative actions and where the minority hold the majority of the wealth, ended up with violence or oppression for the minority such as apartheid in SA, the Chinese in Indonesia and Indians in Kenya. The only sad part is that the uneducated, minority Indians are being left behind economically in the economic battle between the rich, established minority vs the poorer, up and coming majority race.

I was born & bred in Malaysia, left at age 15 (1980) because the unequal rights of Malays/Muslims & non-Malays (non-Muslims). It got so bad that 80% (if not more) of my generation in similar socio-economics left, for the same reasons. We knew there was next to no chance of us getting into local universities, gov't jobs, sort after business permits, lucrative gov't contracts, etc. There are about 40 of us in my generation (first cousins only), all those beyond high-school education (>30) were educated overseas & didn't return for good. All, except one, graduated with at least one degree & landed great jobs globally. We all established roots & took citizenship elsewhere. BTW, Malaysia does not allow dual citizenship. My father/uncles used to say kids in my generation, who chose to remain in Malaysia was either of super wealthy family (able to buy their way through life) OR have no way out (can't afford/not smart enough for scholarship). We were told to leave the comfort of home as soon as were deemed mature enough. The well intended Affirmative Action clause for Bumiputras/Malays in the Constitution when negotiating independence from Britain was miss-used (surprise! surprise!), resulting in a country with "two tier of citizenship rights". Things have improved over the years because the economy has improved since the discovery of oil. But it's still pretty bad, or the Brain Drain won't be so drastic & on-going.... The gov't is doing all it could, trying to entice Malaysians like me to return (loyalty to your birth country, fast track career, financial rewards, etc). So far, no one I know took the offer. A few wanting to live in Malaysia (with aging parents) just use the avenues mentioned in the video & kept their foreign passport!!! Visiting "back home" gets increasingly less as the older generation passes on. 

If you are a white foreigner from a wealthy country living among the "well to do's" in Malaysia, you won't be exposed to all this ugliness because it's a privilege lifestyle, any social issues or unjust will be nicely tucked away.......  

Do visit Malaysia because it is a beautiful country & wonderful as a foreigner. I'll swing by for its amazing food when I escape the cold for a few weeks this Winter. Every time I hear locals griping of the hidden/non-spoken "affirmative action" of Malaysia, I'm sooooo glad to call Canada home, and my loyalty shall ALWAYS stay with Canada & my fellow Canadians. I feel so blessed ❤🥰👍.

The downside for me is the time zone. I have lived too many years in Canada. When I finally escape, I want 300 days of sunshine and blue skies each year. I find Asia just too cloudy for much of the year.

I will still happily visit, but I don't know that it is a place to live.

It's worth mentioning that the highway (motorway) networks thruout the Peninsular are good and well maintained. Easily one of the best in the world. Not the Klang Valley tho' as roads are terrible these days. I recently had to nip down to JB from PJ for a funeral - it was an incredibly smooth and almost effortless 3.5 hours drive. On the way back up, as the PLUS Highway was chock-a due to the Xmas/New Year/School Hols, I decided to take the old roads from Gelang Patah all the way up. Was amazed at how well the old roads are kept, it took many more hours for sure but was pretty cool to pass thru towns like Pekan Nenas (Pineapple Town), Batu Pahat (Chisel Stone), Muar and Melaka etc.. Muar is fantastic it's so clean, historical, laid back and the food's good. Once the rail network is more on par with the likes of East Asia and Europe, this will present more options for easy and frequent travel between states. And if the High Speed Rail ever gets completed between KL and Singers - bada bing bada boom!

Thank you so much for letting us know that you thought our video is useful. Really appreciate the feedback! Malaysia is a very good option for solo travelers, you will feel very comfortable there. Not only do most people in Malaysia speak english (even paper work is in english) but you will meet a lot of friendly people. You can stay for three months straight in Malaysia and if you want to extend the visa again for 3 months you can do a boarder crossing to Singapore or Thailand. Buses and trains work very well in Malaysia. 
Malaysia is a good starting point if you are digital marketing. You will get a lot of inspiration here and learn a lot of new things. 
As for food....enough choice, don't worry about not finding anything you like. Rice and noodles are often the substitute for patato but you can easily explore new dishes in the many food courts often located on the top floor in the malls. Try the roti canai (bread) it is delicious! Have fun planning your trip to Malaysia and lots of luck once there! Matt and Monico

1. Choose a town/city that has

i.   a highway passing nearby
ii.  rail with frequent connection to KL

5. With USD3,500, you'll lead an upper middle class life in Malaysia.

1. Choose a town/city that has

i.   a highway passing nearby
ii.  rail with frequent connection to KL
iii. good private hospitals
iv. private schools
v.  easy connection to KLIA, by train/s or flight.
vi. 5G.
vii. A university that allows the public to be a member of its library.

2. The requirement for a foreigner to purchase a house cannot be RM1 million. In some towns, there are no houses that cost that much. This condition should vary from state to state.

3. If you decide to live outside KL, you will need a car. A good cheap one is the Myvi.

4. You'll need medical insurance to get a long term visa.

5. With USD3,500, you'll lead an upper middle class life in Malaysia.

Malaysia Petrol Ron 97 is 5.15 ringgit,  Singapore Grade 98 is $3.97, don't currency exchange!

one uniqlo shirt, same pattern  in Singapore is $29.90, Malaysia is 129.90 ringgit,  which is cheaper?

one Galaxy S26, Malaysia is 6800 ringgit ，Singapore is $2128. If both earn their own currency 3000 ringgit and  singdollar respectively per month. Singapore only need half a month to get the phone,  whereas in Malaysia,  you need two months to get the same phone.

Thank you for your video about my country.
I am a Malaysia, Chinese have lived here for decades and have seen Malaysia rise, fall, and rise again. Every challenge we have faced has been part of our journey as a growing nation. We don’t develop overnight like Dubai.…..we grow gradually, at our own pace, with our rich and diverse culture.

I’m truly grateful to our Ministry of Education for shaping an education system since the 60s that exposes our children to world history from an early age. Our kids may only know bits and pieces, but they understand there is a wide world out there with different countries, climates, languages, and geographies.

Let me share this…..back in the 80s, some Americans joked that Malaysians still lived in trees …… we just laughed! We have always known about the world outside of Malaysia... it’s just that the world didn’t know much about us. Funny, isn’t it?

Here in Malaysia, we live humbly and peacefully. All we want is stability and decent jobs. Want to be rich, just work harder, maybe work 2 jobs and do a small business like selling Malaysia burgers (many of our youngsters doing that…) Thanks to our government and NGOs, there are CSR support everywhere  no one truly goes hungry here, except maybe a few who choose not to work (which happens everywhere). Even with a minimum wage of around RM1,700, you can still live modestly without going hungry because of the subsidies on essentials like rice, sugar, cooking oil, petrol……you name it….we are lucky.

Of course, some Malaysians move abroad, just personal preference, some enjoy it, many come back home eventually. That’s just part of life. No government is perfect (politics will always be noisy!), but the best thing we can do is stay united and focus on living harmoniously among the PEOPLE down here….

And here’s one thing about Malaysians …..we may argue among ourselves like siblings, but if an outsider criticizes us, we all unite in a heartbeat. We have gone through good and bad times together, and that is the bonding that we build. We only want a peaceful home to live in.

Looking back, I still believe Malaysia is one of the best countries to live in. Sure, I’ve had my share of personal struggles, but nothing changes how I feel about Malaysians are some of the kindest, most humble people on Earth.

We are patience…..you notice we don’t honk unnecessarily when we are driving, we can wait in long Qs for food without complaint, and we even enjoy traffic jams with snacks and coffee! Everyone here is treated equally; no big hierarchies, no arrogance, any status can just jam at ‘mamak’ for ‘the tarik’ we are all the same because we share the same roots, same food, same education in a diverse cultures….the Malay, Chinese, Indian, Sabahan, Sarawakian,…there are more..

There’s so much more I could say, but to sum it up:
I LOVE YOU, MALAYSIA and I love my fellow Malaysians. ❤

I've been to KL three times and would go back every year if I could. It's excellent value for money and the climate once you get used to the humidity is perfect. Lovely people who speak English and great food.
However there are some cons which strangely expats who make YouTube videos never mention.
Drivers generally do not give way to pedestrians. Many drive straight through red lights. I nearly got run over on a pedestrian crossing by a guy texting on his phone! Crossing roads there is risky business and could lead to your dream vacation being cut short if you don't have your wits about you!!!Despite the glossy veneer it remains third world. You often see and smell piles of rubbish and litter in the poorer areas even within a couple of blocks of Bukit Bintang. At times you are greeted with the smell of raw sewerage despite the fact you can if you hold your nose  think you were in Singapore. Definitely a city of contrasts.
And as a caution I would advise against eating street food considering that I caught food poisoning eating at a five star hotel. Despite the cons it's one of my favourite destinations in Asia but do   be careful crossing roads even at the lights.

I have lived in KL ~25 years since 1986. I mostly agree with Andrew's statements in this video, but feel like I should share some of my experience;
Property prices; $500k is indeed upscale (well, you can buy $5mill penthouse with 3 floors and your own swimming pool in the sky), but it is possible to get a new decent apartment for $100-150k, even a house if you are prepared to do some renovation (laborers don't earn a lot). All depends on which area, and distance to city center.
Food prices; All about "where". In the video clip, Andrew is walking around in the Bukit Bintang area, which is expat and tourist-y. Food prices there are 3-5 times higher than in the local suburbs (Cheras, Ampang, Kepong/Selayang,...). "Downside" with the local suburbs is that no fancy western food exist at all.
Dating; Used to be great. Went downhill in the late 90s, and probably an art form or pure magic by now.
Business; A lot of the business community is orbiting "clubs", meaning Selangor Golf club, Jockey club, Yacht club, Freemasons/Rotary, and not night clubs. If you are a member elsewhere in the world, it may get you in the door and if accepted, connections get a lot easier. Once "inside" the community, a lot of informal "visiting" at offices happens to expand the network and if you have a few really good friends, they tend to want to introduce you to many others on such "visits" -- don't turn those down.
Corruption; I have never bribed an police officer, and others seem to do it often. Never figured out why? In business deals, it is unfortunately common but not always. I recommend that you set things up so that it becomes invisible to you, and have a trusted broker that handles all of that.

I've lived in Singapore for 16 years and Malaysia is just across the causeway from here.
Never lived there but have worked there on assignments and visited it frequently.

I wonder if you would have liked it when I first visited in the late 80s.
It wasn't full of endless malls, and many still lived in kampongs.
Somehow I liked it better then.  It had more individuality and with less crowding and highrise.
Yet I like Singapore more now than during the same period.
I tend to find a characteristic sameness about the modern parts of Malaysia, though some areas are quite distinct such as KLCC..
Though I'd argue the same about many cities, not just KL.

Malaysia's population is 23% Chinese and much of the industry and trade is controlled by people who are ethnically Chinese.
And obviously, much of the food, art an culture is also Chinese or mixed (what here is called Peranakan).
On the other hand, Singapore is 76% Chinese. 

Yet the races are really not that united.
Since Mahatir's "Malay Dilemma", non-Malay (Bumiputra) people have been actively discriminated against.  Some improvements have been made since then, but the country is far less united than is Singapore.
Further, political corruption, cronyism, incompetence and self-interest are ever-present, and the people just accept that whoever is in power will continue with the same ways.
I can't tolerate that.  I also lived in Indonesia for some years ago, and it was at least as bad.

The cost of living is low largely as incomes are low by Singaporean or western standards.
GDP per person is around USD14,000 in 2025, and basic wages are RMB1,700 a month, which is only USD350 or so.
Yes, there are malls and western shops everywhere but the average workers tend to shop elsewhere.
The times I have worked in Malaysia, I have found it frustrating and sometimes annoying.
From a western perspective, workers were not as dedicated and focused as i hoped.  That does not apply to everyone, but it applies to a lot.
Getting things done was not a simple matter, and I found that I had to waste too much time supervising others, many whom were professionals and well educated.
I've never met anyone that doesn't speak English in Malaysia

Sorry, looking at Singapore vs Malaysia costs & salaries should not look at the currency exchange rate, which mainly impact asset value of money deposits in long run. Most goods & services that are portable are internationally priced, including salaries. I had worked 25 years ago in Korea & Germany as "cheaper labor" from Singapore, then Malaysian salaries had already been much lower than Singapore. But as Singapore economy grew be more competitive, now matches and some areas even higher than Korea, Japan & Germany, while Malaysia's economy had been lagging behind after "currency controls following AFC", become somewhat uninvested.
Anyway to benchmark salaries, should look at recruitment agency salary guides like Michael Page, Robert Walters, and Randstad, then could really compare salaries especially for white collar, professional, specialist jobs. 
So to estimate how much you could earn in Singapore, let's use another way since you know many Singaporeans in your line. Yourself as Financial advisor, influencer... perhaps if good enough (performance) could match your Singaporean peers that could own a condo & car, and assuming could get close to that salary. That's like top 20% income earner (83% population stay HDB, 17% in private housing). So would you try for job that could perhaps bring you at least MYR$62k? Fruit for thoughts. And that's why many people, around the world, wants to work in Singapore.
In 2000, top 20% earns monthly from S$8,000 or MYR18.4k or USD4.6k (based on 2000 exchange rate). 
Now in 2024,  would earn S$20k or MYR$62k or USD15.6k (USD to SGD exchange rate not change so much)

I’m a Taiwanese and have been living here in KL for more than a year. The overall price level is low (transportation/food/housing,etc) but it all comes with a cost. 

1. Low Food quality. The weather has been too harsh for food to be kept safe and fresh. Four out of ten times (4/10) I got rotten yogurt from a top tier supermarket’s fridge. Hygiene is sometimes an issue (e.g.) water shortages happens occasionally. Besides, people near me always coughing/sneezing somewhere, which got me COVID once.

2. Bad transportation. Traffic jams everywhere and by no means you can avoid that, cuz public transportation seems non-existent, cuz it can’t get you where you want. Even if it does, it’s neither reliable nor stable , high MKBF meaning it gets late often.

3. Better be careful if you’re a non-Muslim single woman. Religion seems overly emphasised to me here in KL. If you love eating pork, don’t like children, dress like a modern lady, and well educated (in a westerner way), then your life will be somewhat difficult in an invisible way.

4. People are generally really nice but super inefficient. For example, I applied for a credit card in a local bank and still haven’t got it yet (still in progress), after a 5-month wait. Yes five months. Just unbelievable in the western world. Better not go grocery shopping during weekends since your milk will prolly go rotten while you wait in a long queue.


All in all, I don’t recommend KL to be a perfect place for the rich who pursues life quality. The income level here in KL limits the possibilities - you can’t easily get “premium” service (tranquil/swift/direct/fresh/hygienic/organic), even if you’re rich. For me, the life here in KL is a bit challenging in my perfectly honest opinion.

We need not be a professional alarmists to observe that Malaysia’s steady march toward Islamic conservatism represents something rather more sinister than mere cultural evolution. These are not mere political parties in the conventional sense, they are vehicles for the imposition of seventh-century Wahabi jurisprudence upon a diverse, modern society. That such movements gain traction by promising to police hemlines and enforce gender apartheid tells us everything we need to know about the intellectual poverty at their core.

The notion that Malay identity must be wedded to an increasingly Wahabi interpretation of Islam is as historically illiterate as it is culturally destructive. @JohnFlores-tj1mb  Bangsar, Damansara Heights and KLCC. The best areas by a million miles.
the no tax on foreign earnings is pretty clever; it creates a source of foreign currency flowing into the country.

Retirement visas are costly in West Malaysia and getting healthcare insurance is steep or non existent if you have a lot of pre existing conditions. Also you need to have a certain pension requirement

You can live in KL for way less than 3000 usd a month. Im renting a one bedroom apartment in a modern complex with all the amenities over in Mont Kiara which is a more premium suburb, for under 2100MYR per month, which includes all utilities and internet. 
No need to waste money on a vehicle as grabs (uber) is very cheap, if you only need to commute every other day or so.

So after that, all you need is money for food. I eat a lot, and i can easily spend less than 50MYR per day eating out, i could probably get by with 25MYR if i really wanted to. 

So 1500MYR per month on food eating out for every meal, and 2100MYR on rent and bills per month comes to around 3600MYR for all the essentials, which ends up being about 910USD a month. If you can earn 2000k USD per month, you can easily save half of that and live a comfortable life, and not pay any tax.

I dont know why there arnt more people doing it.

Keep in mind, this is for a single person.

But you could also rent a bigger place for the same price in a less premium area, which is still very clean and very safe. So you could still potentially raise a family quiet cheaply. And if you buy groceries instead of eating out you could save even more.
